createdBy: RDX Comprehensive Headlight TARA Example v1.0
sources: ISO/SAE 21434:2021 - Road vehicles Cybersecurity engineering - Annex H
ISO/SAE PAS 8475:2023 - CAL and TAF framework
UN/ECE WP.29 R155 - Cybersecurity management system

itemDefinition.title: Adaptive Front-lighting System (AFS)
itemDefinition.boundary: Adaptive headlight ECU including dual-core MCU, light beam control logic, stepper motor drivers, LED matrix controller, CAN interface, LIN bus, UDS diagnostic stack, ambient light sensor, and vehicle speed sensor input; boundary extends from OBD-II connector to headlight mechanical actuators
functions: Adaptive beam control based on steering angle and speed
Glare-free high beam with camera feedback
Diagnostic communication via UDS over CAN
Secure firmware update via OTA or dealer tool

architecture.description: The AFS system is built around a dual-core MCU (COMP-ECU) connected to the vehicle CAN network via a security gateway (COMP-CAN-GW). The ECU communicates with light actuators (COMP-ACTUATOR), reads sensor data (COMP-SENSOR), and exposes diagnostic services through an OBD-II port (COMP-OBD). A CAN network segment (COMP-CAN-NET) interconnects all electronic nodes.

components.name: Headlight ECU
components.description: Dual-core Aurix TC3xx MCU running AUTOSAR-compliant headlight control software. Manages LED matrix, stepper actuators, and communicates via CAN and LIN.

components.name: Body Domain Gateway
components.description: Central gateway ECU that filters and routes CAN messages between body domain and other vehicle domains. Enforces message authentication for safety-critical frames.

components.name: OBD-II Diagnostic Port
components.description: Physical OBD-II connector providing external access to vehicle diagnostic services. Entry point for authorized workshop tools and potential attacker physical access.

components.name: Body CAN Network
components.description: CAN FD bus at 500 kbps interconnecting body domain ECUs including headlight ECU, BCM, and gateway. No encryption at network layer.

components.name: Headlight Beam Actuator
components.description: Stepper-motor-based actuator assembly controlling horizontal and vertical beam direction. Receives commands from headlight ECU via LIN bus.

components.name: Ambient Light and Speed Sensor Interface
components.description: Sensor cluster providing ambient light level and vehicle speed input to the headlight ECU. Speed signal sourced from ABS ECU over CAN.

dataFlows.name: Light Control Commands (CAN)
dataFlows.dataDescription: Body Control Module light command frames: beam direction, intensity, mode selection

dataFlows.name: Vehicle Speed Signal (CAN)
dataFlows.dataDescription: Vehicle speed in km/h sourced from ABS ECU, broadcast on body CAN

dataFlows.name: Actuator Position Commands (LIN)
dataFlows.dataDescription: Stepper motor position setpoints for horizontal and vertical beam adjustment

dataFlows.name: UDS Diagnostic Session (OBD-II)
dataFlows.dataDescription: Diagnostic requests: read DTC, read data by identifier, security access, firmware update

dataFlows.name: Gateway Filtered CAN Traffic
dataFlows.dataDescription: All body CAN traffic routed through gateway for filtering and routing

interfaces: CAN-Body (500 kbps CAN FD, body domain)
PWM-LEDs (direct PWM output to LED matrix driver)
itemDefinition.environment: Automotive production vehicle, global markets, temperature range -40 degrees C to +85 degrees C, IP67 enclosure, exposed to vibration and humidity per LV124
assumptions: Vehicle CAN network is separated from external networks by security gateway
Physical access to vehicle interior requires vehicle break-in or owner consent
OBD-II port access is physically restricted in production vehicles
Dealer diagnostic tools are authenticated before security-critical operations
Attacker has no prior cryptographic material for message authentication
Firmware update signing keys are protected in HSM with no extractable private key material

damageScenarios.title: Driver or Other Road Users Blinded by Incorrect Headlight Operation
damageScenarios.description: An attacker causes the headlight ECU to disable forward lighting or produce maximum intensity in the wrong direction, resulting in loss of driver visibility or blinding of oncoming traffic, creating conditions for a serious traffic accident.
impactedFunctions: Adaptive beam control based on steering angle and speed
Glare-free high beam with camera feedback
references: ISO 26262:2018 ASIL-B safety requirement for lighting system
ECE R48 - Installation of lighting and light-signalling devices

damageScenarios.title: Persistent Oncoming Traffic Glare from Uncontrolled High Beam
damageScenarios.description: The glare-free high beam function is disabled or overridden, causing continuous full-intensity high beam illumination that blinds oncoming vehicle drivers, increasing collision risk.
impactedFunctions: Glare-free high beam with camera feedback

damageScenarios.title: Covert Vehicle Location Tracking via Speed Data Monitoring
damageScenarios.description: Unauthorized passive monitoring of vehicle speed signals on the CAN bus, combined with GPS or road-map correlation, enables covert tracking of vehicle movements and driver behavior over time without consent.
impactedFunctions: Adaptive beam control based on steering angle and speed
references: GDPR Article 9 - Special categories of personal data
UN/ECE WP.29 R155 Annex 5 threat list item 5.4

damageScenarios.title: Vehicle Identity and Usage Pattern Exposure via Diagnostic Data
damageScenarios.description: Extraction of VIN, odometer readings, DTC history, and configuration data from diagnostic services reveals vehicle identity and usage patterns, enabling profiling, targeted theft, or insurance fraud.
impactedFunctions: Diagnostic communication via UDS over CAN

damageScenarios.title: Complete Headlight System Failure Due to Corrupted Firmware
damageScenarios.description: Malicious firmware modification causes total loss of all headlight functions including low beam, high beam, cornering lights, and dynamic leveling, leaving the driver without forward illumination during night or adverse weather conditions.
impactedFunctions: Adaptive beam control based on steering angle and speed
references: ISO 26262:2018 ASIL-B - headlight system safety goal

damageScenarios.title: Persistent Vehicle Network Compromise via Headlight ECU as Pivot
damageScenarios.description: A compromised headlight ECU running attacker-controlled firmware is used as a persistent foothold to conduct lateral attacks against other vehicle systems including powertrain, braking, or telematics, enabling ongoing surveillance or safety-critical manipulation.
impactedFunctions: Diagnostic communication via UDS over CAN
Secure firmware update via OTA or dealer tool
references: UN/ECE WP.29 R155 threat category T.10
Miller & Valasek Jeep Cherokee remote exploit research

threatScenarios.title: CAN Message Injection to Manipulate Light Control
threatScenarios.cause: Attacker gains physical access to the vehicle CAN network via OBD-II port or exposed harness and injects malicious light control command frames, overriding legitimate BCM commands to cause dangerous lighting behavior.
references: CAN bus injection research: Miller and Valasek 2015
ISO/SAE 21434 Annex H threat scenario H.TS.1

threatScenarios.title: UDS Diagnostic Service Exploitation for Data Exfiltration
threatScenarios.cause: Attacker obtains physical access to OBD-II connector and uses stolen or reverse-engineered diagnostic credentials to authenticate a UDS session, extracting sensitive ECU data or initiating unauthorized firmware operations.
references: ISO/SAE 21434 Annex H threat scenario H.TS.2

threatScenarios.title: Passive CAN Monitoring for Speed Data Interception
threatScenarios.cause: Attacker installs a covert CAN data logger device (e.g., via OBD-II port or harness tap) that passively records speed and positional correlates for long-term behavioral tracking of the vehicle and driver.
references: GDPR threat model for connected vehicles

threatScenarios.title: Headlight ECU Firmware Modification for Persistent Compromise
threatScenarios.cause: Attacker exploits weaknesses in the secure boot chain or firmware update authentication to upload malicious firmware to the headlight ECU, creating a persistent backdoor or deliberately damaging safety-critical software.
references: ISO/SAE 21434 Annex H threat scenario H.TS.4

attackSteps.title: Gain Physical Access to Vehicle Interior
attackSteps.description: Attacker breaks into or otherwise obtains access to the vehicle interior to reach the OBD-II port or an exposed CAN harness connector under the dashboard.

attackSteps.title: Connect to OBD-II Port or Exposed CAN Bus
attackSteps.description: Attacker plugs a standard CAN adapter (e.g., USB-to-CAN or ELM327-compatible device) into the OBD-II port or a tapped harness connector, gaining direct access to the body CAN network.

attackSteps.title: Identify Headlight Control CAN Message IDs
attackSteps.description: Attacker uses CAN sniffing software to record traffic and correlates changes in headlight behavior with specific CAN frame identifiers, identifying the light control command messages.

attackSteps.title: Inject Malicious Light Control CAN Messages
attackSteps.description: Attacker replays or crafts malicious CAN frames with the identified headlight control message IDs, overriding legitimate BCM commands to set beam direction, intensity, or mode to dangerous values.

attackSteps.title: Access Diagnostic Connector with Rogue Tool
attackSteps.description: Attacker connects a laptop or custom diagnostic device to the OBD-II port using a standard J2534 interface to initiate a UDS communication session.

attackSteps.title: Establish Authenticated UDS Extended Diagnostic Session
attackSteps.description: Attacker uses stolen or brute-forced security access seeds and keys to bypass UDS security access and open an extended diagnostic or programming session with the headlight ECU.
references: UDS security access algorithm reverse engineering research

attackSteps.title: Extract Diagnostic Data, VIN, and ECU Configuration
attackSteps.description: Within the authenticated session, attacker reads ECU data via ReadDataByIdentifier (0x22), reads stored DTCs, and extracts VIN, calibration data, and hardware/software version identifiers.

attackSteps.title: Obtain Firmware Update Access via Programming Session
attackSteps.description: Attacker escalates the UDS session to a programming level session, enabling firmware erase and download services by exploiting weak security seed algorithms or previously extracted credentials.

attackSteps.title: Bypass Firmware Signature Verification
attackSteps.description: Attacker reverse-engineers the firmware signature verification implementation, identifies a vulnerability such as a missing check or a weak algorithm, and crafts a payload that passes signature validation.
references: Secure boot bypass research - automotive ECU

attackSteps.title: Upload Malicious Firmware Image to Headlight ECU
attackSteps.description: Attacker uses the TransferData and RequestTransferExit UDS services to upload a crafted firmware binary containing backdoor or sabotage payloads to the ECU flash memory.

attackSteps.title: Establish Persistent Backdoor in ECU
attackSteps.description: Malicious firmware activates a persistent backdoor that survives normal ECU resets, providing attacker ongoing access to vehicle network from the headlight ECU as a pivot point.

attackSteps.title: Install Covert CAN Data Logger for Passive Speed Monitoring
attackSteps.description: Attacker installs a small CAN data logger (OBD-II dongle or inline harness device) that passively records all CAN traffic including periodic speed messages broadcast by the ABS ECU.

attackSteps.title: Correlate Speed Data Patterns with Location and Build Movement Profile
attackSteps.description: Attacker retrieves logged speed data and correlates it with known road network characteristics, timestamps, and optional GPS data from a co-located device to reconstruct vehicle movement history and build a surveillance profile.

attackPaths.title: CAN Network Physical Access Leading to Light Control Manipulation
references: ISO/SAE 21434 Annex H attack path H.AP.1

attackPaths.title: Diagnostic Access Leading to Data Exfiltration
references: ISO/SAE 21434 Annex H attack path H.AP.2

attackPaths.title: Diagnostic Access Leading to Firmware Modification and Persistent Compromise
references: ISO/SAE 21434 Annex H attack path H.AP.3

attackPaths.title: Passive CAN Monitoring Leading to Vehicle Movement Profile Construction
references: ISO/SAE 21434 Annex H attack path H.AP.4

riskThresholdMatrix.rationale: High attack feasibility means the attack is very achievable; only reduce or avoid are acceptable treatments per organizational policy.
references: OEM cybersecurity policy doc CP-001 v3.2

riskThresholdMatrix.rationale: Medium feasibility attacks may be treated by reducing attack surface, avoiding the risk through design change, or sharing risk through contractual means.
Low feasibility attacks may be accepted if residual risk is within tolerance and documented justification is provided.
Severe impact scenarios involving potential fatalities require mandatory risk reduction or elimination.
Major impact requires management review and treatment strategy justification.
Moderate impact allows full range of treatment strategies with appropriate documentation.
Negligible impact scenarios may be accepted without treatment.
Critical risks must be escalated to CISO and program management before any treatment decision.
High risks require documented treatment plans reviewed by cybersecurity officer.
Medium risks allow full treatment range with justification.
Low risks may be accepted with documented rationale.
Very high TAF indicates the attack is within reach of motivated organized adversaries; mandatory treatment required.
High TAF requires treatment strategy with documented rationale.
Medium TAF permits full treatment range.
Low TAF scenarios may be accepted or shared with documented justification.
Very low TAF scenarios are considered theoretical and may be accepted.

attackFeasibilityRatings.rationale: CAN injection requires physical access (hours to achieve) but uses standard off-the-shelf tools and publicly documented CAN protocols; no specialist reverse engineering needed.
Requires specialized diagnostic tools and knowledge of OEM-specific UDS security access seed/key algorithm; expertise level raises overall feasibility score.
Firmware modification requires significant reverse engineering of secure boot chain, custom exploit development, and bespoke tooling; estimated effort in days to weeks.
Passive CAN monitoring requires only brief physical access to install a commercially available OBD-II logger; subsequent data analysis requires moderate expertise.
Gaining access to vehicle interior is a well-understood physical security problem requiring no technical expertise.
OBD-II CAN adapters are commercially available and widely documented; connecting to bus is straightforward.
Identifying headlight CAN IDs through traffic analysis and behavioral correlation requires moderate time but is achievable with open-source tools.
CAN frame injection using identified message IDs is achievable with standard tools once IDs are known.
J2534 pass-thru interfaces are commercially available; connecting to OBD-II and starting a UDS session is well-documented.
Bypassing UDS security access requires knowledge of OEM-specific seed/key algorithms, which are restricted and require reverse engineering effort.
Once authenticated session is established, data extraction via standard UDS services is straightforward for someone with diagnostic expertise.
Obtaining programming session access requires advanced UDS knowledge and OEM-specific credentials or exploiting a vulnerability in the security access mechanism.
Bypassing firmware signature verification requires deep knowledge of the cryptographic implementation and significant reverse engineering with custom tooling.
Uploading custom firmware assumes signature bypass has been achieved; the upload process itself follows standard UDS protocols.
Establishing persistence in ECU firmware is the final step after upload and requires deep understanding of AUTOSAR runtime and flash memory management.
Commercial OBD-II data loggers are freely available and trivial to install; no technical expertise required beyond plugging in a device.
Correlating speed data with road network and timeline requires moderate analytical skill but is achievable with open-source mapping tools and publicly available road data.

impactRatings.rationale: Loss of forward illumination or blinding of driver constitutes a safety-critical condition directly causing potential fatalities; severe safety rating dominates overall impact score.

Persistent glare to oncoming traffic creates a significant accident risk and potential regulatory violation; rated major safety impact rather than severe as the vehicle's own driver is not directly blinded.
Long-term movement tracking constitutes a major privacy violation under GDPR but poses limited direct safety or financial harm; privacy category drives the rating.
Diagnostic data exposure reveals vehicle identity but has limited immediate actionable impact compared to movement tracking or safety scenarios.
Complete headlight failure in low-visibility conditions creates an extreme safety hazard equivalent to total lighting failure; also triggers costly recall and regulatory action.
Using the headlight ECU as a pivot enables attacks on safety-critical systems; multi-category major impact reflects broad blast radius of persistent compromise.

riskValues.rationale: Medium attack feasibility (physical access required but using standard tools) combined with severe safety impact (potential fatalities) results in a High risk rating requiring mandatory treatment.
Medium feasibility (requires specialist tools and expertise) with limited privacy impact yields Medium risk; treatment focused on access control is sufficient.
Medium feasibility (brief physical access needed) with major privacy impact results in Medium risk; monitoring controls and GDPR compliance measures address the threat.
Low feasibility (requires weeks of expert effort with bespoke tools) despite major multi-category impact; strong firmware signing controls reduce residual risk to acceptable level.

controls.title: CAN Message Authentication Code (MAC) via CANSec or AUTOSAR SecOC
references: AUTOSAR SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication) specification R22-11

controls.title: UDS Diagnostic Security Access with Cryptographic Challenge-Response
Secure Boot with Asymmetric Firmware Signature Verification
references: NIST SP 800-193 Platform Firmware Resiliency Guidelines
Infineon Aurix secure boot application note

controls.title: CAN Payload Encryption for Sensitive Messages
Diagnostic Request Rate Limiting and Intrusion Detection
references: ISO 14229-1 response pending and timing parameters
AUTOSAR Intrusion Detection System Manager (IdsM) specification

riskTreatmentDecisions.justification: High safety risk classification mandates reduction. CAN message authentication prevents injection of forged light control commands by requiring cryptographic MACs; payload encryption further prevents message replay. Combined controls are projected to reduce the attack feasibility band from Medium to Low.

Cryptographic challenge-response security access prevents unauthorized diagnostic sessions; rate limiting detects and throttles brute-force seed attacks. Together these controls reduce feasibility to Low band and bring residual risk within tolerance.

The privacy impact of passive speed data monitoring is rated as Medium risk. Physical access is required to install a monitoring device, and the vehicle already benefits from physical security measures. The cost-benefit analysis of CAN encryption for all speed messages does not justify the implementation effort given the residual risk level and existing physical access restrictions.

Firmware signature verification via secure boot prevents execution of unsigned firmware images. Diagnostic access control prevents unauthorized programming sessions. These controls address both the upload mechanism and the execution of malicious code, reducing the residual risk to Low band.

calAssuranceLevels.title: Cybersecurity Assurance Level 1 - Basic
calAssuranceLevels.description: CAL 1 represents basic cybersecurity assurance appropriate for components with low cybersecurity risk. Activities focus on documenting security requirements and performing basic testing.
objectives: Basic vulnerability analysis has been performed
Security testing covers basic functional validation
Residual risks are identified and documented
assuranceActivities: Threat analysis and risk assessment (TARA) documentation
Code review for obvious security issues

calAssuranceLevels.title: Cybersecurity Assurance Level 2 - Enhanced
calAssuranceLevels.description: CAL 2 represents enhanced cybersecurity assurance for components with moderate cybersecurity risk. Activities include structured design analysis, security architecture review, and systematic testing.
objectives: Security architecture is formally documented and reviewed
Attack feasibility ratings are systematically derived
Security testing includes structured penetration testing
Security design patterns are applied and documented
assuranceActivities: Systematic TARA with traceability to security requirements
Structured penetration testing with documented methodology
Static analysis and code scanning with security rulesets

calAssuranceLevels.title: Cybersecurity Assurance Level 3 - Rigorous
calAssuranceLevels.description: CAL 3 represents rigorous cybersecurity assurance for components with high cybersecurity risk affecting safety-critical functions. Activities include formal methods, advanced penetration testing, and independent verification.
objectives: Formal security proofs or model-based analysis are applied to cryptographic implementations
Advanced penetration testing including hardware attacks is performed
Supply chain security for security-critical components is verified
Continuous monitoring and incident response capabilities are demonstrated
Independent third-party security assessment has been completed
assuranceActivities: Formal security model verification for cryptographic protocols
Hardware-level penetration testing including side-channel analysis
Supply chain integrity verification with signed bills of materials
Fuzz testing of all external interfaces
Red team exercise against production representative system
Continuous vulnerability monitoring with defined SLAs

calAssessments.assessor: Automotive Cybersecurity Assessment Team, TUV Rheinland
calAssessments.rationale: SecOC implementation has been reviewed and functional testing passed at CAL2 level. Formal verification of the MAC algorithm implementation and hardware side-channel resistance testing required to achieve CAL3. Remediation plan established targeting CAL3 by SOP-1.

calAssessments.assessor: Internal Cybersecurity Validation Team, OEM Security Lab
calAssessments.rationale: UDS security access implementation uses ECDH-based challenge-response with 128-bit entropy. Penetration testing confirmed resistance to brute-force and replay attacks. CAL2 activities completed including structured pentest, static analysis, and independent review.

calAssessments.assessor: Automotive Cybersecurity Assessment Team, TUV Rheinland
calAssessments.rationale: Secure boot chain verified through formal cryptographic protocol analysis and hardware penetration testing including fault injection and side-channel analysis. Third-party assessment by TUV Rheinland confirmed CAL3 requirements met. Supply chain for HSM components verified.

calAssessments.assessor: Internal Cybersecurity Validation Team, OEM Security Lab
calAssessments.rationale: Rate limiting implementation tested and confirmed functional. Basic security testing completed. AUTOSAR IdsM configuration reviewed. CAL1 activities sufficient for this defensive depth control.

tafAssessments.rationale: CAN injection attack requires physical vehicle access but employs readily available tools and publicly documented techniques. Organized crime groups with automotive expertise (e.g., thieves, insurance fraudsters) have the motivation and means to execute this attack. The safety impact on public road users elevates the target value. Physical access requirement limits the attack surface but does not make it negligible.

Firmware modification is a sophisticated attack requiring nation-state or advanced persistent threat (APT) actor capabilities. The attack demands weeks of preparation including reverse engineering the secure boot chain and developing bespoke exploitation tools. However, the persistence and lateral movement potential make this highly valuable for espionage or sabotage operations against high-value targets. Physical access requirement significantly limits the attack population.

Passive tracking attack has the lowest attacker skill bar of all scenarios: commercial OBD-II logging devices costing under 50 euros are sufficient. Once installed, no further interaction is needed. The threat is relevant for stalking, insurance fraud, theft planning, and targeted marketing. High score reflects low complexity despite physical access requirement.

relationships.justification: CAN message injection directly compromises the integrity of light control commands, the primary asset targeted by this threat scenario.
Diagnostic exploitation directly targets the confidentiality and integrity of diagnostic information stored and transmitted by the ECU.
Passive CAN monitoring directly targets vehicle speed data confidentiality by capturing broadcast speed signals.
Firmware modification attack targets firmware integrity as the primary asset, enabling persistent compromise.
Successful CAN injection of malicious light control commands can disable forward lighting or set maximum intensity, directly causing driver blindness or oncoming traffic glare.
Injected commands overriding glare-free high beam logic can sustain maximum-intensity illumination in the direction of oncoming traffic.
Unauthorized diagnostic data extraction exposes VIN, DTC history, and usage patterns constituting vehicle identity information.
Collected speed data correlated with road network information enables covert vehicle location tracking over time.
Malicious firmware replacing legitimate headlight control code can cause complete loss of all lighting functions.
Persistent backdoor firmware enables ongoing access to vehicle network for lateral movement and surveillance operations.
CAN MAC authentication prevents injection of unsigned frames; attacker cannot forge valid MACs without knowledge of the symmetric key stored in ECU HSM.
Payload encryption prevents replay attacks by making captured frames undecodable without the session key, complementing message authentication.
Cryptographic challenge-response security access prevents unauthorized diagnostic sessions; brute-force attacks are infeasible within the rate-limiting window.
Rate limiting detects anomalous diagnostic request patterns and throttles responses, preventing automated brute-force of security access seeds.
Secure boot verifies each firmware component signature at startup; unsigned or tampered firmware is rejected and the ECU enters a safe fallback mode.
Programming session security access prevents unauthorized initiation of firmware update sequences; an attacker must bypass this before attempting to upload malicious firmware.
Asymmetric signature verification on every boot ensures only OEM-signed firmware can execute, directly protecting firmware integrity.
CAN MAC authentication protects light control commands from injection or replay, preserving integrity of the asset.
Security access control restricts reading of sensitive diagnostic identifiers and ECU configuration data to authenticated sessions.
The CANSec/SecOC implementation is designed to meet CAL3 assurance requirements, currently assessed at CAL2 with remediation underway.
Secure boot implementation fully satisfies CAL3 assurance level requirements as confirmed by third-party assessment.
UDS security access implementation meets CAL2 assurance requirements as confirmed by internal validation team.
CAL assessment CALA-CAN-AUTH formally evaluates the achieved assurance level of the CAN authentication control.
CAL assessment CALA-FW-VERIFY formally evaluates the achieved assurance level of the firmware verification control.
CAL assessment CALA-DIAG-ACCESS formally evaluates the achieved assurance level of the diagnostic access control.
CAL assessment CALA-RATE-LIMIT formally evaluates the achieved assurance level of the rate limiting control.
The firmware modification attack path contains the persistent backdoor establishment step as its final stage.
The CAN injection attack path contains the malicious frame injection step as its primary attack action.
The CAN injection attack path specifically targets the body CAN network segment to inject malicious frames.
The diagnostic exploitation attack path targets the OBD-II port as the entry point for unauthorized diagnostic sessions.
CAN message authentication for safety-critical light control commands requires CAL3 assurance level per organizational policy and risk rating.
Firmware signature verification protecting against persistent compromise requires CAL3 due to the high impact rating of firmware tampering.
Third-party assessment by TUV Rheinland confirmed CTRL-FW-VERIFY meets all CAL3 assurance requirements.
Internal validation assessment confirmed CTRL-DIAG-ACCESS meets all CAL2 assurance requirements.
Diagnostic exploitation is a prerequisite attack path that enables firmware tampering; a successful diagnostic exploit significantly increases the feasibility of firmware modification.
A persistent compromise of the ECU could be leveraged to subsequently cause headlight malfunction, linking these two damage scenarios in a multi-stage attack chain.
Both risks relate to the integrity of the headlight ECU's operational commands; treating one may influence the residual risk of the other.
